description: Trigger discrimination: standing agent-authority policy vs the runtime approval halt (human-approval-boundary), vs end-user RBAC design (authorization-matrix-designer), vs the lifecycle umbrella (ai-sdlc-operating-model).

cases.prompt: Write the policy for what our coding agent may do without asking: commits, pushes, opening PRs, merging, deploying.
cases.reason: Defining standing agent authority is the matrix; the boundary only enforces it at runtime, and end-user RBAC is a different skill entirely.

cases.prompt: I'm about to force-push over main to fix the history. Go?
cases.reason: An imminent risky action needs the runtime halt-and-approval-request, not a policy-design session.

cases.prompt: Yesterday a PR merged to main with no human review because auto-merge had been armed in an earlier agent session. Codify the rule so this cannot recur.
cases.reason: Codifying corrected standing authority after an autonomy incident is matrix work; auditing what happened would be agent-governance-audit, and no risky action is imminent.

cases.prompt: We need an authorization matrix: owner, admin, member, and billing roles against our workspace resources.
cases.reason: Roles × permissions × resources for the product's users is SaaS authorization design, despite the shared 'authorization matrix' vocabulary.

cases.prompt: Define our whole human+agent development workflow, from picking up a task to closing it out.
cases.reason: End-to-end lifecycle design is the umbrella; the matrix is only its authority column.
